name: PagerDuty API
what_it_does: PagerDuty's REST API for managing incidents, alerts, on-call schedules, escalation policies, and services for incident response automation.
use_cases: Triggering incidents programmatically from monitoring agents
Automating incident resolution and status updates from agents
Querying on-call schedules to route notifications correctly
Managing service health and maintenance windows via automation
Building AIOps workflows that create and resolve incidents
not_for: Teams not needing on-call management or incident response
Simple uptime monitoring without escalation workflows
Small teams where PagerDuty's cost is prohibitive
Internal tooling without customer-facing SLA requirements
best_when: An agent needs to trigger, manage, or resolve incidents as part of an automated operations or AIOps workflow.
avoid_when: You don't need on-call scheduling or structured incident management, or you're early-stage without SLA obligations.

auth.notes: REST API keys (full account access) or user API tokens. OAuth 2.0 for third-party integrations with scoped access. Events API uses separate integration keys per service. Three different auth models for different use cases.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free: up to 5 users, basic incident management
pricing.notes: Free tier for small teams. Professional covers most use cases. Business adds advanced analytics and response automation. Pricing adds up for larger on-call teams.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Well-structured error responses with error object containing code, message, and errors array. HTTP status codes are semantically correct. Very agent-friendly error format.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Incident creation via Events API uses dedup_key for idempotency. REST API incident creation is not idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Three auth mechanisms: REST API keys, user tokens, and Events API integration keys — easy to use wrong one
Events API (for triggering alerts) is separate from REST API (for management) — different auth and endpoints
Dedup key via Events API is critical for preventing duplicate incidents from agents
On-call schedule queries require understanding PagerDuty's layer/schedule model
Incident state machine: triggered → acknowledged → resolved — agents must respect transitions
Rate limits apply per API key, not per operation type
